items.title: The games industry is stifled by a culture of fear
items.summary: A workshop at Madeira Games Summit concluded that maintaining courage in your convictions is the key to successful game development
items.content:
A workshop at Madeira Games Summit concluded that maintaining courage in your convictions is the key to successful game development

Image credit: Playdead Feature by Lewis Packwood Features Editor Published on July 24, 2026 The games industry is stifled by a culture of fear that prioritises algorithmic trends and market research over the creative conviction and personal taste required to produce genuine hits.

That was the conclusion of a workgroup hosted by developer and consultant Rami Ismail at the 2026 DevGAMM Madeira Games Summit, which took place in May. The Vlambeer co-founder's workgroup – entitled "We're moving fast and losing what made us good (creativity, craft & the future)" – called for the industry to reject risk-averse decision-making, asserting that the reflexive avoidance of failure has become a greater threat to success than the inherent uncertainty of creative work.

Ultimately, the group concluded that unique products are forged through internal coherence and disciplined validation, rather than "sanding down the edges" to appeal to everyone.

The workgroup consisted of an eclectic mix of creators, producers, communicators, publishers, and service providers, and the group's goal was to discuss the pathways to reestablishing creativity as a central tenet of game development and game funding processes, with the idea that current incentives are misaligned with creativity.

Disproportionately successful titles such as Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, Peak, and Balatro were given as examples of games driven by personal conviction and individual taste that no market analysis would have predicted. Hence, the workgroup's thesis was that the games industry creates its best works outside of market research – and that while data is a powerful tool, taste is what drives breakout hits.

This was contrasted against the reflexive need to "chase the algorithm" or "make games for everyone" – mechanisms aimed at reducing risk at the expense of creative conviction.

The workgroup highlighted four pillars that are key to a creatively centred game design approach. The first was creating coherence , which was defined as creating works that do not contradict their own thesis or goals. But forging games with internal consistency requires conviction, time, and continuous validation.

The second pillar was identifying weaknesses at a product, studio, and personnel level, while the third was selling the dream . The latter applies both internally and externally, and the group was in strong agreement that many creatives and leads – at both the AAA and independent level – are failing to sell the dream of the game through communication and motivation.

The fourth pillar, connecting the dots , involved connecting the disparate inputs and incentives into structures that allowed the other three pillars to succeed.

One of the key points to emerge from the discussion was that stagnation is an unavoidable outcome of a fearful industry, and that creative conviction is required to maintain a studio or individual's creative identity. However, picking a lane and excelling in it does not require a singular genre, theme, or even target demographic – although it does require knowing who you are and what you stand for.

The workgroup acknowledged that commercial success should be a goal for any commercially developed title, but argued that imposing additional arbitrary, non-creative pressures for success creates diluted work. The group also recognised that taste is important, but is inherently divisive – it necessarily involves distinguishing an "in-group" from an "out-group." In other words, those that like something and those that do not.

Another key takeaway was how creative people often find it difficult to make tough decisions, including letting go of their games. Studios can spend tremendous resources seeking validation and chasing ideas that are not good, or that do not fit the current moment, rather than making a decisive choice early on. The group concluded that the only thing worse than making a wrong decision is making no decision when one is needed.

In relation, the workgroup found that studios and individuals often do not ask for help when it's needed, which could prevent avoidable mistakes and unlock further opportunities. The participants acknowledged that the games industry is a deeply interdisciplinary, innovative, and iterative field, and no one person or team holds all the answers.

items.title: Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 & the Beauty of a Non-Definitive Ending
items.summary: Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 gives Maelle and Verso two painful endings. This feature examines grief, escapism, and why neither choice is wrong.
items.content:
Imagine: it’s PAX West 2025, and you’re me, wandering the expo hall with hundreds if not thousands of others. It’s a sea of people, but amongst them, you start to notice an incredibly peculiar pattern: dotting the crowd are bright red French berets. You cross direct paths with someone and notice their red beret is not only accompanied by a black-and-white striped shirt, but a red checked sash, strapping a fake plastic baguette to their back. You’re immediately both intrigued and perplexed, and raise the obvious question to your partner, who explains it’s from that game he started playing “with Charlie Cox in it”.

In this scenario, you are very much a fan of Charlie Cox and his starring in Daredevil, so you’re immediately reminded of what he’s talking about in that explanation – Clair Obscur: Expedition 33. You vaguely remember seeing the first casting announcement trailer for it during a random State of Play one early morning and getting excited because Daredevil(!), but ultimately you ended up going back to your endless, hellish cycle of Marvel Rivals lobbies when the game actually released. The red berets are a kind reminder of your previous excitement about the game, though, and it isn’t long after that you find yourself glued to your television while being serenaded by a beautiful French accent as you are formally introduced to the citizens of Lumiere. Your four years of French classes throughout school surely prepared you for this moment.

What you aren’t prepared for is just how much of an effect this game has on you by the end.

Now, at the risk of being honest, it’s a complete understatement to say I love this game. If you have interacted with me within the last year, I have talked to you about this game, or I have told you to finish it after you told me you didn’t even get through Act I, or I have endlessly gushed over Lorien Testard’s soundtrack what was probably an annoying amount. It’s rare to find yourself so committed to a piece of media, if only because you see yourself reflected in it so wholly, whether it’s in Maelle’s escapism or Verso’s self-sacrificial tendencies. After all, you don’t win 37 awards because people just feel pretty okay emotions about your game. As is with any piece of media, though, comes what others have to say about it.

(Obligatory note: What’s discussed going forward is riding off of the notion that the person reading this has played the game. If you haven’t, maybe go open Steam or the PlayStation store, grab some tissues, strap in for a bit, and hopefully you will thank me later. Or feel free to keep reading – I’m certainly not stopping you).

One of the gaming community’s most divisive conversations regarding Expedition 33 is regarding the two distinct endings the game has to offer, notwithstanding how both are unique in how they highlight the game’s central theme around the idea of grief. There hasn’t been a day that’s gone by in recent memory where I haven’t been faced with someone explaining how “[insert your preferred character’s name] ending is the right one”, or “if you chose [insert your not-preferred character’s name] ending, you’re clearly heartless”.

In the first ending, the audience is brought back to Lumiere after Maelle and Verso’s battle, and we see that Maelle has not erased the canvas, which was her motivation by the end of Act III. We’re rejoined by the ensemble we’ve grown to love, the Expeditioners as well as Esquie and Monoco, and we even see that Gustave and Sophie have both been repainted after their previous deaths. We’re also faced with Maelle and a considerably older Verso. We see Verso struggle for an uncomfortable moment, his hands stretching out and freezing over keys of a grand piano in Lumiere’s opera house, until it can be discerned Maelle uses her Paintress abilities in order to force the painted version of her late brother to play.

The colors are washed. The lighting is dimmed. It doesn’t yield much comfort aside from the embrace of familiar faces, but that just might be enough.

The second ending is an intentionally stark contrast. For the first time in the game, we’re officially brought outside of the canvas now that it’s presumably been destroyed, as that was Verso’s desire by the end game, and we’re shown the Dessendre’s. In front of them sits a grave donning the name “Verso Dessendre”. Renoir and Aline share a tender embrace in front of their son’s grave, Clea leaves behind a bouquet of achingly familiar red and white flowers before excusing herself, and we see a scarred Alicia– or Maelle– standing by as she watches the scene unfold, holding a sewn Esquie doll. The camera turns and we see the images of the very same Expeditioners, including Gustave and Verso’s painted self, framed by the Eiffel Tower as they wave goodbye before dissipating.

The colors are bright. It’s backlit by a sunny day. The comfort is uncertain and bittersweet, but it’s ultimately not an unwelcome feeling, either.

Undeniably, the discussion around these endings is, of course, typically viewed as black-and-white as possible. More often than not, when faced with multiple endings by the end of a game, audiences are used to getting a “good” ending and a “bad” ending, leaving little room for discussion. And, in these cases, that is the game’s intent. In these cases, the games do have a black-and-white, a good-and-bad. It’s not unheard of for a game to punish a player for making poor choices throughout a play through. When looking at Expedition 33’s endings, though, this mindset is frankly doing a disservice to the game’s theming as a whole.

Grief as a theme is interwoven throughout the entire game, and when is the process of grieving ever black-and-white? Grief by all accounts is an incredibly non-linear process. We have all grieved over someone, over something, and we all understand what that means. Both of the endings evoke very different emotions, but it really is up to the player and what they choose to prioritize within their own grief. Yes, in Maelle’s ending, we see the way she chooses to use her Paintress abilities against her brother and father’s wishes, but more importantly, she’s also surrounded by those she has come to love, in a fully-functioning body. She’s given grace to those who have only ever lived inside of the canvas, reasserting the idea that they are sentient individuals with their own lives and autonomy, despite the circumstances they were painted into. Within Verso’s ending, the Dessendre’s are now able to work on moving past their shared grief regarding the loss of their real-life brother and son after years of stagnation, but at the cost of Alicia having a healthy physical body, and at the loss of everyone we have come to love inside of the canvas.

Both of these endings offer a resolution and catharsis in their own ways, and the outcome of one doesn’t make the other more or less right. When discussing these endings, we can’t assume why someone chose one ending over the other, because their grief surrounding the game is ultimately their own, and it’s not up to us to dictate how that feels to them. It can’t be stressed enough how sacrificing nuance in these discussions doesn’t only do a disservice to the game, but also the players and their own emotions about it.

I have a confession to make: I wholeheartedly chose Maelle’s ending the first time I played, and at that moment, it wasn’t a difficult choice for me. It made sense. I had grown so incredibly fond of Lumiere and the canvas and those inside of it, just like Maelle, but I saw myself in so many facets of her. I was a sixteen-year-old girl once, finding escape in worlds I created and in the media I was in love with, be it novels or games or movies, in order to remove myself from the very real grief I was experiencing. It’s something I still find myself doing even in adulthood, and ironically, probably the reason why I found myself so absorbed in Expedition 33 in the first place. The hours I put into the game were the direct result of me needing a distraction from life, and to watch the game slowly begin to unveil what it truly was felt like looking into a mirror. Of course I chose to save Lumiere. Of course I sided with Maelle. It’s what I do all the time. More often than not, I run from my grief rather than let myself feel it, and instead I look for reassurance and acceptance and comfort in worlds where my real feelings can’t hurt me. It’s far easier to feel for the characters on my screen than it is to feel for myself. After all, their emotions aren’t tangible, unlike my own. Expedition 33 was the first time I had ever personally experienced a piece of media that conveyed that feeling in a way that felt so specific, and so undeniably real.

items.title: Best character builds in Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
items.summary: This Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 best build guide lays out two builds for each character, covering offense, defense, support, and hybrids.
items.content:
The best builds for Lune, Maelle, Sciel, Verso, and Monoco in Expedition 33

Image credit: Rock Paper Shotgun/Sandfall Interactive Guide by Josh Broadwell Contributor Additional contributions by Jeremy Blum Published on July 22, 2026 Follow Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 Putting together Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 builds often feels overwhelming. Each character has access to skills that have so many different, potent combinations with other skills or other character builds, and that's not even getting into the hundreds of Pictos you can augment your setups with.

For the most part, you can get through the main story and most optional bosses on normal mode without bothering to optimise builds, though some late-game foes will put this strategy to the test. Expedition 33's Expert mode is another matter entirely, though. Enemies frequently one-shot you there, and you'll need to stack the deck in your favour as often as possible.

In this guide, we lay out two builds for the characters in Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 , covering offence, defence, support, and hybrids. Most of these builds work just fine on their own without needing a complementary build from a different character, though we've noted when some of them function better with a certain ally. We've also listed Pictos for each build and where to find them.

Lune is a brilliant attacker or supporter, depending on what your party needs. And even in her support role, she's free to dish out plenty of free aim and skill damage thanks to an expensive, but essential, skillset. An attack-focused Lune has several skill combinations, but it's also easy to overwhelm yourself with too many high-AP abilities and get caught with a deficit more often than not. The right combination includes a handful of low-cost skills to keep Lune's AP topped up and a regular rhythm of powerful attacks.

Lune's support build depends on Typhoon, a mid-game healing skill that activates at the start of a turn and heals the entire party for a substantial amount. If Lune has two Earth Stains, Typhoon consumes those and activates at the start of every turn for three to five turns. Basically, you'll get free healing and ice damage, though it takes a bit of set-up to reach that point.

We've added Terraquake to this build to get Lune those two initial Earth Stains, but from there on, the Benesim weapon does the job for you. At level 10 and higher, it generates one Earth Stain at the start of each turn, so by the time Lune needs to re-cast Typhoon, she'll have plenty of Stains. The starting perk reduces healing skill costs by one AP as well.

Throw in all the support Pictos listed above, and Lune will be buffing the entire party and giving them a fair bit of AP every turn while also buffing herself (though do note that Energizing Heal gives others AP, not Lune).

All of this leaves Lune with a lot of time and AP to deal damage. Use Wildfire to burn enemies, then Thermal Transfer to damage them and gain extra AP. If Thermal Transfer consumes two Earth Stains, it also lets Lune have another turn. Just bear in mind that extra turns gained this way or through the Cheater Picto count toward Typhoon's turn total.

Stormcaller, Crustal Crush (good for Breaking enemies), and Mayhem are Lune's main sources of damage. Stormcaller is particularly good with Wildfire, since consuming two Fire Stains makes Stormcaller summon extra lightning strikes. Regardless of Stains consumed, Stormcaller deals additional damage when other allies attack foes affected by it.

If you're playing in a team of AP-hungry characters like an offense-focused Maelle or Sciel, you might want to put Energizing Powerful and Shell on Lune as well. These grant 2 AP to the allies receiving the buffs — but not Lune.

This build functions similarly to the support build, just without all the buffs, and pairs well as a sub-DPS with Maelle, Verso, or even Sciel. Set foes alight with Wildfire, then use Thermal Transfer to build AP. Crustal Crush is excellent for building Break, whether you've got another Break character in the party or not, and Mayhem is an exceptionally powerful skill — and an expensive one. Revitalization is here to keep the party's health topped up.

Just make sure to keep up a steady rotation of alternate element skills. Kralim's perk increases skill damage by 20% up to five times, but the buff resets if you use the same element twice.

Maelle is one of Expedition 33's strongest characters, though working around her Stance switching — and costly skills — can take some getting used to. She's an excellent debuffer who can burn enemies and lower their defense, though while Maelle does have some support options, it's best to ignore them. Let another character buff Maelle, so she can focus on wiping the floor with your foes, and we've got two builds below that do just this, with one focused on DPS and the other on counterattacks.

items.title: Jennifer English, star of Baldur's Gate 3 and Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, leaves Tides of Annihilation lead role
items.summary: English, who won a BAFTA award for her work on Clair Obscur in 2026, says the decision was difficult but
items.content:
English, who won a BAFTA award for her work on Clair Obscur in 2026, says the decision was difficult but "personally necessary."

When you purchase through links on our site, we may earn an affiliate commission. Here’s how it works .

(Image credit: John Phillips/BAFTA/Getty Images for BAFTA) Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 12 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter Jennifer English, who gained fame for her portrayal of Shadowheart in Baldur's Gate 3 and then won a BAFTA award for best performer in a leading role for Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, has announced that she is withdrawing from the role of Gwendolyn in the upcoming Tides of Annihilation in order to "properly look after [her] mental and physical health."

Revealed in 2025, Tides of Annihilation struck me as an Arthurian Elden Ring set in modern-day London : Gwendolyn, the only survivor of the "Outworld invasion" that's shattered London, can summon the spirits of the Knights of the Round Table to help her do battle with the "demi-gods of Avalon." As videogames go, I'd say it sure looks very videogame-y .

Watch On No points for originality there, but it's among the 50 most wishlisted games on Steam , and that's not nothing. But the game suffered a blow when developer Eclipse Glow Games announced that English was stepping down.

"Earlier in June, Jennifer informed us that she would no longer be able to continue in the role," the studio wrote on X . "While we were, of course, saddened by her decision, we completely understood and respected Jennifer in prioritizing her well-being. We are also deeply grateful for the care and responsibility she showed throughout this process.

"During this transition, Jennifer remained incredibly supportive and, together with other members of our voice cast, helped us identify and connect with talented performers who could carry Gwendolyn forward while staying true to the character."

Baldur's Gate 3 cast 'changed the game' for videogame actors, says Dragon Age: Inquisition lead: 'We too deserve to stand up and be counted' Andy Serkis says 'there has always been that snobbery' when it comes to videogame acting, but the times are changing thanks to games like Expedition 33 D&D-inspired Critical Role's homegrown videogame is coming along well, says Laura Bailey: 'There's a lot of work being done' English offered some insight into her decision in an Instagram story that was subsequently shared on Reddit , saying that she needs to "slow down and reassess the commitments" she's taken on.

"After careful thought, I've realized I need to take time to rest and properly look after my mental and physical health," English wrote. "With that in mind, I've made the difficult—yet personally necessary—decision to step away from [Tides of Annihilation] as it moves into its next phase.

Keep up to date with the most important stories and the best deals, as picked by the PC Gamer team.

"During this transition, I was pleased to help introduce the Tides of Annihilation team to a very talented actor who I believe is a wonderful fit for Gwen. Having seen her passion, talent, and care for the craft, I'm excited for players to hear her interpretation of the role and can't wait for players to experience what she brings to Gwen."

English added that she is "happy to remain involved with Tides of Annihilation as an advisor."

Eclipse Glow has selected a new actor for the role of Gwendolyn, who is now recording the English performance for the Tides of Annihilation hands-on demo that will be playable at Gamescom. Neither the studio nor English divulged the name of the new actor, however—I'm guessing that will be revealed when the demo debuts in August.

items.title: New Official Clair Obscur Release Confirmed for 2027
items.summary: Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 developer Sandfall and its partners showcase a new release focused on Gustave coming in 2027.
items.content:
New Official Clair Obscur Release Confirmed for 2027 From GameRant (Written by Joshua Duckworth) on July 16, 2026 | OpenCritic

Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 has become one of gaming's biggest success stories over the past year, and fans will be able to revisit it in 2027 with a brand-new release. This new release is focused on the best Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 character Gustave, although fans should be aware that it is on the pricier end.

The award-winning RPG quickly earned a passionate fanbase after its breakout launch, and since then, Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 has remained on everyone's tongue. Everything from merch to new updates has been on the menu for a while, but now fans have something else to look forward to— Clair Obscur: Arm of Gustave .

Guillaume Broche, director of Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, recently made some salient and noteworthy comments about Zelda: Breath of the Wild.

The newest collectible to join the world of Expedition 33 is Clair Obscur: Arm of Gustave, a life-size replica of Gustave's iconic mechanical arm produced by PureArts. Measuring 19 inches long, the premium collectible faithfully recreates one of the game's most recognizable objects with a marble-like polyresin finish, metallic gold accents, rotating cylinders, and a detailed display base covered in flowers. It's one of the most elaborate gaming collectibles...

items.title: Expedition 33 lead says if people don't like the studio's next game 'that's life'
items.summary: Sandfall Interactive isn't trying to make another GOTY
items.content:
By Willa Rowe Published Jul 2, 2026, 1:05 PM EDT News Sandfall Interactive doesn't seem too worried about living up to GOTY expectations

After GOTY pull, Clair Obscur devs draw line in sand over AI: 'Everything will be made by humans by us' 2026's most anticipated indie horror game just got delayed Final Fantasy 7 Revelation datamining has fans hopeful for DLC Expedition 33 lead says if people don't like the studio's next game 'that's life' Image: Sandfall Interactive/Kepler Interactive Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 Sign in to your Polygon.com account Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 , the debut game from developer Sandfall Interactive, was a smash hit in 2025 and earned numerous game of the year awards . The studio's next project, whatever it may be, might not be as popular. Director Guillaume Broche knows Clair Obscur is a tough act to follow, and he's okay if no one likes what the studio does next.

In a lengthy episode of Video Game Club on the Konbini YouTube channel, Broche discusses Sandfall's next game and says, "Maybe people won't like it. That's life." It is a surprisingly laid-back attitude toward following up one of the most beloved games in recent memory. For Broche, it isn't any different from the ethos he and the rest of the team at Sandall Interactive had while making Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 . "We didn't make the first game to please anyone," he confesses, "and I think that's why it worked." Rather than pleasing an imaginary audience, Broche reveals that development on Clair Obscur was focused on satisfying the developers. They made a game that excited them. It was just an added bonus that plenty of players enjoyed the game as well.

Guillaume Broche accepts the Game of the Year award at The Game Awards 2025. Image: The Game Awards The process obviously worked, and Broche sees no reason to mess with it, despite mounting expectations brought on by the success of Clair Obscur . He admits that there is definitely pressure to make things bigger or to expand the studio because of the attention Sandfall Interactive now has for its next project. Ultimately, Broche says that this would only result in the studio making games that he doesn't actually want to play.

As for Sandfall's next project, Broche declined to share details but did reveal the team will make some drastic decisions. A big question is whether or not the studio's next game will be a sequel to Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 . In the interview, Broche reiterates the idea he's shared before that "Clair Obscur" is the name of the franchise and Expedition 33 is just one story the team wanted to tell. This could mean Sandfall will take an anthology approach like in Final Fantasy , with each entry introducing a new world and cast of characters. Returning to the world of Expedition 33 in a true sequel would also require Sandfall to choose a canon ending for the game, something the studio has deliberately avoided. In any case, whatever follows Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 will be worth watching.

Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 is available on PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X/S, and Windows PC.

A recent interview session between Twitch streamers and content creators saw Sandfall Interactive clarifying its AI usage

items.title: Clair Obscur’s Director Knows Kingdom Hearts II Is The Best One
items.content:
Kingdom Hearts is back in the broader public consciousness (we know it never leaves the minds of sickos) with Kingdom Hearts IV getting a new trailer last month , and that means it’s as good a time as any to remind you that Kingdom Hearts II is the best one . 20 years later, the PS2 classic remains a pitch-perfect summation of the best parts of Square Enix’s Disney and Final Fantasy mash-up. If you don’t want to take my word for it, ask Guillaume Broche, creative director of Game of the Year winner Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 .

Broche appeared on an episode of Jeux Vidéo Club, French YouTube channel Konbini’s show in which people walk through a video game store and tell stories about the games that inspired them. In it, Broche talks about how Kingdom Hearts II is one of his favorite games.

“I think [ Kingdom Hearts II is] in my top 5 games of all time,” he says as translated through Konbini’s video translation. “Truly exceptional. […] There’s really something about that gameplay that I’ve been looking for ever since Kingdom Hearts II . Kingdom Hearts III is pretty good, but it didn’t manage to recapture everything [ II ] did in terms of gameplay, which was exceptional. There was a real depth to it. You could spend 150 hours in the game, actually. Seriously, if you like action RPGs with cool systems, play this game.”

Though Broche recommends anyone play the game if they like action RPGs, Kingdom Hearts is a series that you probably shouldn’t just jump into with the second entry, even if someone says it’s the best in the series. The series’ 20 years of stories are all deeply intertwined, to the point where Kingdom Hearts IV is including characters from games you can’t even play anymore . Yes, Kingdom Hearts II is the best one, but eat your veggies and play the first game and Chain of Memories first.

Don’t miss the latest reviews, news and tips. Sign up for our free newsletter.

items.title: Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 is now available in Arabic!
items.summary: Expeditioners! Following the huge demand from so many of you, we’ve been working super hard to be able to finally announce that Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, is now available with Arabic text! This brings the total number of supported langua
items.content:
Expeditioners!

Following the huge demand from so many of you, we’ve been working super hard to be able to finally announce that Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, is now available with Arabic text!

This brings the total number of supported languages to 20!

With the new Arabic language support, we had to consider additional changes to the game’s UI to ensure a smooth and enjoyable experience for Arabic-speaking players around the world.

Adding Arabic localization has been a true team effort over many months, from the incredible adaptation and localization work carried out by the linguists, to reworking the vast majority of the user interface for right-to-left reading, all the way through localization testing.

Every team involved brought passion and dedication to making this possible, and we couldn’t be more grateful for their work. We hope this update makes the story feel even more personal to our players.

Today’s update also includes some additional fixes and improvements. Read the full patch notes here!

Thank you all so much for your continued support! <3

Tomorrow comes.

~ The Expedition 33 Team

items.title: Patch v1.5.6 is now live!
items.summary: Expeditioners, Following the huge demand from so many of you, we’ve been working super hard to be able to finally announce that Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 is now available in Arabic! Content New supported language (text): Arabic [/*] Credi
items.content:
Expeditioners,

Following the huge demand from so many of you, we’ve been working super hard to be able to finally announce that Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 is now available in Arabic!

Content

  • New supported language (text): Arabic

    [/*]

  • Credits updated (new linguists, testers and fonts)

    [/*]

Fixes

  • Fixed an issue where applying burn after an enemy has been stunned causes them to be able to act immediately after recovering from the stun.

    [/*]

  • Fixed Cleim weapon level 10 Lumina effect that does not trigger correctly on every instance of area of effect skills.

    [/*]

  • Fixed Martenon’s Lumina effects that do not work if Sciel does not have two consecutive turns.

    [/*]

  • Fixed an issue where Sireso weapon, Level 4 Lumina, and Powerful buff, from Pictos/Luminas, applied at the start of battle may override each other depending on the character's position in combat.

    [/*]

  • Fixed Chromatic Lampmaster's AOE skill missing a sound cue on the jump attack.

    [/*]

  • Fixed Sirene's Widow Ballet missing SFX during second charm attack.

    [/*]

  • Fixed characters in combat that may have displaced heads in Photo Mode if they were not seen while turning on Photo Mode.

    [/*]

  • Various minor fixes of the UI and localizations.

    [/*]

We are aware of a crash affecting some of you when loading into the Reacher or going out of the Manor. This crash is related to some specific PC configurations. Until we fix this issue, you can work around it by setting ‘Global Illumination’ to anything below ‘Epic’ temporarily.

As always, thanks for your support and for your reports via the official Typeform - We'll continue monitoring the reports coming in and working on future fixes!

~ The Expedition 33 Team

items.title: Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 lead says Zelda: Breath of the Wild was "the first open-world game that actually delivered on the promise of an open world"
items.content:
"It's truly exceptional," says Guillaume Broche

When you purchase through links on our site, we may earn an affiliate commission. Here’s how it works .

(Image credit: Nintendo) Copy link Facebook X Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 15 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter It's no secret that Breath of the Wild broke Nintendo's usual Legend of Zelda formula with its expansive open-world take on Hyrule – but it didn't just revolutionize the decades-old series.

According to Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 creative director Guillaume Broche, it also transformed the open world genre in its entirety. The RPG lead explains as much in a new episode of Video Game Club via Konbini on YouTube (below), first recalling his introduction to The Legend of Zelda as a child. "I played Zelda when I was really little on the Game Boy," says Broche. "And the only other Zelda game I've played is Breath of the Wild."

Watch On This sets the director apart from the rest of Sandfall Interactive, as he points out "we have a lot of big Zelda fans on the team" – but he was impressed by Breath of the Wild, to say the least. "Generally speaking, I'm not really into open-world games," as Broche describes. "It's literally the first open-world game I've ever liked." He wasn't sure he would, though. "When I started playing it, I was really reluctant."

He continues, detailing how he went on to "force" himself to play Breath of the Wild because "everyone was saying it was good." And his verdict? "It's truly exceptional. In my opinion, it's the first open-world game that actually delivered on the promise of an open world – of simply not having a minimap. You have a big map, but you really get that sense of exploration. You see something, and you want to go there."

And, you can. Or, you can do something else – the world is truly open, in that sense. "Ten thousand things happen along the way, and you get sidetracked. You never end up where you want to go because you keep getting sidetracked by all sorts of things." As Broche concludes, "In terms of level design, it's an absolute masterclass." I can certainly agree there, as a longtime Zelda fan myself. Breath of the Wild and Tears of the Kingdom are wonderful.

Final Fantasy 14 had best relaunch in "history of video games," Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 dev says Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 devs were told that "no one" would like one of the RPG's best systems Shigeru Miyamoto decided Zelda dungeons aren't "really that much fun" while making Ocarina of Time Sure, they break free from Nintendo's beloved Zelda formula – but I'd say that's what makes them so brilliant. They serve as the perfect stepping stone into the series' setting (and apparently, open-world games in general), while also presenting veterans with a fresh take on Hyrule. I know that not everyone feels the same here, but they're undeniably gems. Heck, take a look at their Metacritic scores … even compared to other Zelda entries.

I do wonder what Broche will think of the Ocarina of Time remake now – or whether or not he'll play it at all, considering his previous stance on Zelda. Personally, I hope the Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 director gives it a go.

Weekly digests, tales from the communities you love, and more

Baldur's Gate 3 and Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 show "RPGs are as vibrant" now as ever, Obsidian lead says, even if the genre can look "a little tired" sometimes.

After spending years with her head in various fantastical realms' clouds, Anna studied English Literature and then Medieval History at the University of Edinburgh, going on to specialize in narrative design and video game journalism as a writer. She has written for various publications since her postgraduate studies, including Dexerto, Fanbyte, GameSpot, IGN, PCGamesN, and more. When she's not frantically trying to form words into coherent sentences, she's probably daydreaming about becoming a fairy druid and befriending every animal or she's spending a thousand (more) hours traversing the Underdark in Baldur's Gate 3. If you spot her away from her PC, you'll always find Anna with a fantasy book, a handheld video game console of some sort, and a Tamagotchi or two on hand. She has written for various publications since her postgraduate studies, including Dexerto, Fanbyte, GameSpot, IGN, PCGamesN, and more. When she's not frantically trying to form words into coherent sentences, she's probably daydreaming about becoming a fairy druid and befriending every animal or she's spending a thousand (more) hours traversing the Underdark in Baldur's Gate 3. If you spot her away from her PC, you'll always find Anna with a fantasy book, a handheld video game console of some sort, and a Tamagotchi or two on hand.

items.title: Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 x Dispatch Bundle
items.summary: Expeditioners! Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 and Dispatch are now available together in a 10% off bundle! Bundle art by Derek Stratton This is a really special bundle for us - since its release, Dispatch left such an impact on us and immediat
items.content:
Expeditioners!

Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 and Dispatch are now available together in a 10% off bundle! 

Bundle art by Derek Stratton

This is a really special bundle for us - since its release, Dispatch left such an impact on us and immediately became one of our favourites.   

Our games are quite different, but we couldn’t help ourselves - and ultimately, we just want more people to discover the powerful stories and artistry behind these games.   

So in the words of our Creative Director, Guillaume Broche: “Play Dispatch, it’s very very cool”

Save 10% off both games by purchasing this bundle!

items.title: 1st Anniversary of Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
items.summary: Expeditioners!! Today marks 1 year since we released Clair Obscur: Expedition 33. This past year has been an incredible journey and so we have a few things to share with you today. To celebrate the occasion, the team put together a special
items.content:
Expeditioners!! 

 

Today marks 1 year since we released Clair Obscur: Expedition 33. This past year has been an incredible journey and so we have a few things to share with you today. 

 

To celebrate the occasion, the team put together a special piece of artwork that we hope you will enjoy! 

8 Million Copies Sold

We're so thrilled, and honestly still quite overwhelmed, to announce that Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 has officially reached 8 million copies sold!

Thank you so so much to every single person who has played, created art, shared, streamed, discussed, and supported this game!!

Game Update: New Haircuts + Patch Notes

We've pushed a fun little update live today: new haircuts are now available in-game, giving the expedition even more style to strut around in! 💅 

For the full list of changes, fixes, and improvements, you can read the patch notes here

Community Emotes

Our community is packed with phenomenally talented artists and we wanted to celebrate that today.  

We commissioned some of our amazing community creators to design official Expedition 33 emotes for you to use in your own channels! 

A massive thank you to IrunoArt, comickergirl (Sarah Leuver), and Bugyakis for their brilliant work on each of these!!

They're FREE to download and use here

Official Baguette Merch

In the mood to add some baguette to your wardrobe? 

We’ve partnered with Orcival and Le Béret Français to bring you the official Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 striped shirt and beret for your cosplays! 🥖    You can purchase the official Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 x Orcival mariniere here

And the official Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 x Le Béret Français beret is available to buy here

On top of that, keep an eye out for a limited number of units of the Monolith Set to be back in stock from our partners at Laced and Pix n' Love soon!! 

And Finally… Cake

It's been a big day for us. Thank you for making it possible. 🎂 

And if you know someone who wants to pick up the game, we're running a 20% sale right now on Steam - As well as some fun merchandise goodies available on our merch page!

Merch Page

From the bottom of our hearts, we’re so thankful to each and every one of you – this has been the best year of our lives, thanks to you. 

 

- The Expedition 33 Team

items.title: Patch v1.5.5 is now LIVE!

Expeditioners!

Happy 1 Year Anniversary of Clair Obscur: Expedition 33!! The details of this patch are written out below:

New content

  • Added 1st Anniversary haircuts for the expedition!

    • Gustave's Anniversary haircut is available at the Gestral Merchant near Stone Wave Cliffs.

      [/*]

    • Verso + Maelle + Lune + Sciel + Monoco's Anniversary haircuts are available at the Gestral Merchant near Grosse Tête on the World Map.

      [/*]
    [/*]

Fixes

  • Fixed an issue where Danseuse outfit for Lune and Sciel made the character menu too obscur.

    [/*]

  • Fixed an issue where it was not possible to open a rest point in various locations when an in-world dialogue was playing.

    [/*]

  • Fixed collision issues in various locations.

    [/*]

  • Adjusted some achievement text for consistency.

    • We've noticed something went wrong after adjusting this, we're investigating now to resolve the issue

      [/*]

    • Fixed the issue! We've readjusted some of the achievement descriptions back to the correct achievements. Thank you for your patience!!

      [/*]
    [/*]

As always, thanks for your support and for your reports via the official Typeform - and most importantly, thank you for all of your support this entire year!! We wish you a wonderful anniversary day and hope you enjoy some cake to celebrate with us!

~ The Expedition 33 Team

items.title: The developers of Clair Obscur gave me an interview and some advice
items.summary: We spoke about the game's finale (don't worry, there are spoiler warnings), the best Pictos, and what I can be doing better.
items.content:
Stephen Totilo Mar 27, 2026 35 3 1 Share Clair Obscur: Expedition 33. Screenshot: Sandfall Interactive, captured by Game File This story, perhaps appropriately, goes on a bit longer than you might expect, past what may feel like the conclusion.

This was not by design. In my business of reporting, you can’t plan surprise endings. But it is what delightfully happened.

This story is merely an accurate account of my absurd endeavor from earlier this month, when I tried to finally play through 2025’s most acclaimed video game, the role-playing phenomenon Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 , in a sudden rush.

I had a deadline: a date when I expected to have a chance to chat about Clair Obscur with some of its creators. We would perhaps discuss the game’s ending, which, in their business of making video games, they could plan for.

I will get to all of that. I will get to Thursday night, March 12, when I spoke to two of Clair Obscur’s creators, discussed the ending and got myself an in-person consultation about my characters’ builds.

It’s Sunday, March 8 and I have downloaded Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 to the portable ROG Xbox Ally X , a Switch-looking device that can run games offered via Xbox Game Pass. At this point, I have only played a little past the prologue of Clair Obscur, back on an Xbox in my house. Cloud saving has transferred my progress to the portable. In the game, our protagonist Gustave and his sort-of sister Maelle have set out on Expedition 33, the latest annual beach landing of adventurers who hope they will be the ones to take down the distant and mysterious paintress, who somehow, magically annihilates everyone of a certain age each year.

I know I’ve given myself barely enough time to play through this game ahead of Thursday, when Clair Obscur is likely to win another pile of awards at the former Game Developers Conference (now GDC Festival of Gaming) and when I’ll be backstage with an opportunity to interview the winners.

In the weeks prior, I’ve been busy writing and reporting and, in terms of actually using a controller, playing through Resident Evils .

Just back in February, I had been backstage at a different gaming awards event, where I’d met some of the makers of Clair Obscur and where I’d not spoken to them about the game, because I had other coverage goals for my chats with the winners.

For the Game Developers Choice Awards on March 12, though, I want to talk about the games. My friend urges me on. You should try to finish Clair Obscur, he says. They haven’t spoken about the ending all that much, yet it’s all the game’s fans talk about. You gotta find out what happens, he says, even if you just have to watch a video. Perish the thought.

So I’m on United Airlines flight 1777 from EWR to SFO, comfortably seated in window seat 39A. My Ally X is in my hands, and Clair Obscur is running, well before takeoff. I’ve boarded fairly early. Soon, a man with long curly hair takes the middle seat next to me. There is still plenty of elbow room for me, and I start playing. Best I can tell, the man in the middle seat soon starts a nap.

Gustave, alongside a magically powered woman named Lune, are fighting enemies called Nevrons in dramatic turn-based fights. They reunite with red-haired Maelle. As the plane takes off and begins to cross the United States, things are going well. I’m loving the combat and the look of the game’s world.

Before my plane lands, I reach the end of Clair Obscur’s first act. Big twist! I’ve been rushing, avoiding sidequests, and, wow. I did not see that coming. I’m stunned, but I don’t think anyone on the flight notices. I make sure not to disturb the curly-haired guy next to me, who I think at this point is watching a movie?

After my flight lands, I text my friend to confirm that I have not seen Clair Obscur’s wildest turn. I have not.

items.title: Patch v1.5.3 is live!
items.summary: Expeditioners! Thank you for all of your reports - the details for the fixes in this patch are written out below: Update On Sirene Crash After a thorough internal investigation, the crash that occurred after defeating Sirene can no longer b
items.content:
Expeditioners!

Thank you for all of your reports - the details for the fixes in this patch are written out below:

Update On Sirene Crash

  • After a thorough internal investigation, the crash that occurred after defeating Sirene can no longer be reproduced on our end. If you keep getting this issue, we’d suggest updating your Windows to the latest version. If the issue persists, please submit a report and we’ll continue investigating.

    [/*]

Bug Fixes & Improvements

Photo Mode

  • Fixed facial hair of characters being blurred in the Photo Mode on the close distance if Shading was set to Epic.

    [/*]

  • Fixed an issue in Photo mode that prevented the Inner Cone Angle and Outer Cone Angle options from affecting the shape of the preview light source.

    [/*]

  • Photo Mode's spotlight and point light display has been modified to improve their visibility

    [/*]

Gameplay

  • Fixed an issue where loading a save while picking up a journal could cause that journal to disappear permanently and become impossible to collect again.

    • Missing journals are now also restored retroactively when loading affected saves.

      [/*]
    [/*]

  • Fixed a soft lock that could occur, during the Chromatic Glissando encounter in Sirene’s Dress level, when a dead expeditioner equipped with Shortcut and Second Chance pictos was swallowed.

    [/*]

  • Fixed an issue on Gestral Beach where using the Rematch option after losing the volleyball minigame could create a blocked save.

    [/*]

  • Fixed a visual issue where characters affected by Inverted would not have the correct colour effect applied to their eyes.

    [/*]

Environment

  • Fixed various environment issues (collisions, misplaced assets, missing or incorrect footsteps SFX) across Verso's Drafts, the World Map, and other levels.

    [/*]

  • Fixed various lighting issues on Steam Deck in Verso’s Drafts, Endless Tower and Stone Wave Cliffs levels.

    [/*]

As always, thanks for your support and for your reports via the official Typeform - We'll continue monitoring the reports coming in and working on future fixes!

~ The Expedition 33 Team

items.title: Introducing a new bundle: Metaphor: ReFantazio x Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
items.summary: Introducing a special bundle featuring the award‑winning fantasy RPG “Metaphor: ReFantazio”, created by the team behind Persona 3-5, paired with “Clair Obscur: Expedition 33” - now available at 10% off! Enjoy both titles and experience the
items.content:
Introducing a special bundle featuring the award‑winning fantasy RPG “Metaphor: ReFantazio”, created by the team behind Persona 3-5, paired with “Clair Obscur: Expedition 33” - now available at 10% off!

Enjoy both titles and experience the worlds for yourself!

The Metaphor: ReFantazio × Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 Bundle includes two games: “Metaphor: ReFantazio” and “Clair Obscur: Expedition 33.”

Message from Guillaume Broche & Katsura Hashino

"Metaphor: ReFantazio is a game that is really inspiring for us. It tells a beautiful story in a completely new world that is masterfully crafted. It deals with heavy themes but one that resonates strongly with me is the importance of imagination in all aspects of our lives. The cast is lovable and the turn-based battle system is one of the best I have played. The re-imagination of the traditional jobs system through Archetypes is fantastic and opens up so many playstyles that are fun to play around with. On top of that, the game has one of the most charismatic villains I've ever seen in an RPG. It is a great honor to be able to collaborate with Atlus on this bundle, as I have been a fan of their work since Shin Megami Tensei: Lucifer's Call (also known as Shin Megami Tensei III Nocturne for some players)." - Guillaume Broche

"I am Katsura Hashino from ATLUS, serving as the Creative Director for “Metaphor: ReFantazio.” I am very pleased to hear that a Steam bundle will be released with “Expedition 33,” a title that has received high praise around the world.

“Expedition 33” is a work that conveys a deep understanding of and sincere dedication to the JRPG genre, crafted with great care down to the finest details. As someone who also creates RPGs, I found it to be truly inspiring.

I am genuinely delighted to see Japanese-style RPGs being born and embraced, not only from Japanese-based studios like ours, but other places around the world through diverse creative approaches.

While the two titles differ in their worlds and overall direction, players may find a shared spirit in how we each approach and express the RPG genre. I hope that this bundle encourages players to experience both titles and enjoy the breadth of what RPGs can offer."
